findings.issue: Wave assignment mismatch: Task 5 declares wave:3 but depends_on:[1,2,3,4]. Task 4 is wave:2, so Task 5 should be wave:3 — this is actually correct. However Task 5 depends on Task 3 (wave:1) explicitly, yet the tech-spec wave structure lists Task 5 in 'Wave 2 (depends on Wave 1)' under the label 'WarmupCoordinator + AppModule'. The YAML header says wave:3, which conflicts with the tech-spec section header 'Wave 2'. This is a documentation inconsistency but does not cause an execution error because the depends_on fields are the ground truth. NOT a blocker by itself.
findings.fix: Cosmetic: verify wave numbering is consistent between tech-spec section headers and task YAML headers. Task 5 header wave:3 is correct (depends on Task 4 which is wave:2). Tech-spec's section labeling appears off by one from YAML values.

findings.issue: Task 8 declares wave:6 but depends_on:[1,4,5,6,7]. Task 6 is wave:4, Task 7 is wave:5 — so Task 8 should be wave:6. But Task 9 also declares wave:7 and depends_on:[4,7,8], and Task 10 declares wave:7 and depends_on:[5,6,7,8]. Tasks 9 and 10 are correctly in wave:7 after Task 8 in wave:6. However, Tasks 9, 10, and 11 are all wave:7 and share the same wave — this is correct for parallel execution. No actual cross-task dependency cycle detected. Non-critical note only.
findings.fix: No fix needed — wave numbering is internally consistent when read from YAML depends_on fields.

findings.issue: Task 10 requires WarmupCoordinator.isWarmupInProgress: StateFlow<Boolean> for the TopAppBar Loading state machine (topAppBarState_warmupInFlight_returnsLoading test). This field is NOT defined in Task 5's spec for WarmupCoordinator. Task 5 only defines warmupDefault(), cancelAndRestart(), and the AC-F3 observer. Task 10 explicitly notes this dependency: 'Dependency: this test requires WarmupCoordinator.isWarmupInProgress: StateFlow<Boolean> — this field must be implemented in Task 5'. Task 10 is depends_on:[5,6,7,8] (correctly declares dependency on Task 5), but Task 5 has no mention of isWarmupInProgress in its spec, acceptance criteria, or implementation hints. A consumer task requires a field not defined in the owner task's spec.
findings.fix: Add isWarmupInProgress: StateFlow<Boolean> to WarmupCoordinator's spec in Task 5. It should be derived from the current warmup Job state (true when a Job is active and not complete/cancelled). Update Task 5 Acceptance Criteria and WarmupCoordinatorTest TDD Anchor to cover this StateFlow.

findings.issue: Task 11 calls appSettings.observeDefaultModelId() in ModelManagerViewModel, but Task 2 adds only suspend functions to AppSettingsRepository: getDefaultModelId(), setDefaultModelId(), getLastUsedModelId(), setLastUsedModelId(), isSettingsMigrated(), markSettingsMigrated(). Task 2 does NOT add a Flow-based observeDefaultModelId() method. Task 11's implementation hint reads: 'val defaultModelId: StateFlow<String> via appSettings.observeDefaultModelId().stateIn(...)'. The method observeDefaultModelId() is not in the Task 2 spec and not in the current AppSettingsRepository interface. This is a hallucinated API — Task 11 would fail to compile.
findings.fix: Either (a) add observeDefaultModelId(): Flow<String> to the AppSettingsRepository interface and DefaultAppSettingsRepository in Task 2, or (b) update Task 11 to use the suspend getter pattern: collect via a coroutine that calls getDefaultModelId() and updates a MutableStateFlow. Option (a) is cleaner and consistent with the existing observePerModelSettings() Flow-based getter pattern. Task 2 spec and AC must be updated.

findings.issue: Task 2 modifies DeviceInfoCollector.kt to wire ModelRegistry into AndroidDeviceInfoProvider. The task description says 'add a second constructor constructor(@ApplicationContext context: Context) : this(context, null)'. However, the current AndroidDeviceInfoProvider has only a single @Inject constructor taking Context (confirmed by reading the file). The task adds ModelRegistry as a second constructor parameter to the primary constructor. The secondary constructor is described as 'constructor(@ApplicationContext context: Context)'. But LogExportManager.kt line 73 instantiates it as: DeviceInfoCollector(AndroidDeviceInfoProvider(context)) — i.e., passing Context directly. After Task 2 adds ModelRegistry to the primary constructor, this call site in LogExportManager's secondary constructor would fail to compile unless the secondary constructor of AndroidDeviceInfoProvider is properly defined as constructor(context: Context) : this(context, null). This is feasible but the task's edge-case description correctly identifies it. Flagging as critical because an incorrect secondary constructor definition would break the crash-process log export path silently.
findings.fix: Verify Task 2 implementation carefully adds secondary constructor constructor(@ApplicationContext context: Context) : this(context, null) with nullable ModelRegistry? parameter on the primary constructor. LogExportManager.kt secondary constructor at line 73 must not be modified.

findings.issue: Task 5 (AppModule) describes providing SanctumDatabase via Room.databaseBuilder.build(). Task 6 then wraps that same call in a try/catch corruption handler. However Task 5 explicitly says 'Keep the builder simple for now — do not add fallbackToDestructiveMigration(); Task 6 adds the corruption handler on top of the plain build() call'. This means Task 6 modifies AppModule.kt (listed in its Files to modify) to add the corruption handler. But Task 5 creates AppModule.kt and Task 6 depends_on:[4,5]. This is a sequential modification — correct and expected. However: Task 5 creates AppModule, Task 6 modifies it. Task 6 correctly lists AppModule.kt in 'Files to modify'. No dependency declaration issue here — this is intentional two-task construction. Note: Task 6's AC item 'DefaultDownloadRepository.mainActivityFqn assignment is preserved (no regression)' references a field not visible in the task files read — this should be verified against the actual SanctumApplication.kt.
findings.fix: No fix needed for the two-task AppModule construction pattern. Verify DefaultDownloadRepository.mainActivityFqn is documented in the actual SanctumApplication.kt to confirm it exists.

findings.issue: Task 7 depends_on:[3,6]. However, Task 7 also creates HomeViewModel which uses ModelRegistry.activeModelName (added by Task 3 — declared in depends_on). The dependency on Task 6 is for AppCorruptionState (created in Task 6). But Task 7 also uses ModelRegistry.models with ModelDownloadStatusType.SUCCEEDED for hasDownloadedModels. This field (ModelDownloadStatusType) lives in Model.kt which is not modified by any dependency task of Task 7. This is fine — ModelDownloadStatusType pre-exists. However, Task 7 does NOT declare dependency on Task 0 (Design System) even though the HomeScreen spec says 'use SmSigil (Task 0)' and 'if Task 0 is not yet available, fall back to MaterialTheme tokens with a TODO comment'. This is handled gracefully with the TODO fallback pattern. Not critical because the fallback is explicitly designed into the task. Minor finding only.
findings.fix: Consider adding Task 0 as a soft dependency comment in Task 7 or noting in the implementation that Task 0 artifacts may not be present at task execution time. Current TODO fallback pattern is acceptable.

findings.issue: Task 9 (Drawer UI) depends_on:[4,7,8] but Wave 7 also contains Task 10 and Task 11. Task 9 modifies SanctumApp.kt to plug DrawerContent into the ModalNavigationDrawer (added by Task 7). Task 10 also modifies SanctumApp.kt (via ChatScreen changes). Task 11 also modifies SanctumApp.kt (updates onLoad handler for NavEvent.OpenQuickChat). Three Wave-7 tasks all modify SanctumApp.kt — Tasks 9, 10, and 11 — without any declared dependency between them within Wave 7. They are described as parallel (same wave), but all touch the same file. This creates a merge conflict risk if executed concurrently. No depends_on between Wave-7 peers is declared, which is the standard pattern for parallel tasks, but the shared file modification is a practical conflict.
findings.fix: Document that Tasks 9, 10, and 11 must be executed sequentially (not truly in parallel) because all three modify SanctumApp.kt. Alternatively, restructure so only one task owns all SanctumApp.kt changes. The current setup requires manual merge coordination.

findings.issue: Task 4 depends_on:[1,2] but NOT on Task 3. Task 4 creates DefaultChatRepository which needs ErrorLog (from the existing codebase) and also references ErrorLog.e('history-write', ...) in sweepZombieChats. The 'history-write' component is added by Task 2. Task 2 is correctly in depends_on. However, Task 4's implementation hints say 'ErrorLog.e — suspend-function'. Grep of the actual codebase is needed to verify this. ErrorLog in this codebase is accessed as object ErrorLog.e(...) — checking: the tech-spec verification step for Task 2 says to grep for the new components in ErrorLog.kt. If ErrorLog.e is not a suspend function, the implementation hint is misleading. From reading other task files (Task 5 mentions 'errorlog.e' as a regular call), this seems likely to be a non-suspend static call.
findings.fix: Verify that ErrorLog.e() is actually a suspend function vs a regular function. If it is NOT suspend, Task 4's implementation hint 'ErrorLog.e — suspend-function; in sweepZombieChats vызывать внутри withContext(ioDispatcher)' is misleading but not blocking (can still be called from within a coroutine without suspend keyword).

findings.issue: Task 13 (Code Audit) depends_on:[12] but not on Tasks 13's own audit targets (Tasks 0–11). Wave 9 contains Tasks 13, 14, and 15 all depending only on Task 12. This means audits run after all code is done and docs are updated. The dependency chain is correct. However, Task 13 references ModelEntry.isDownloaded in the focus area description: 'verify Task 4 added AC-F3 observer in WarmupCoordinator: collects registry.models, detects first ModelEntry.isDownloaded=true entry (field added by Task 2)'. But ModelEntry does NOT have an isDownloaded field — it has downloadStatus: ModelDownloadStatus where downloadStatus.status == ModelDownloadStatusType.SUCCEEDED. This is a hallucinated field name in Task 13's description. It could cause the auditor to look for the wrong field name when auditing WarmupCoordinator.
findings.fix: Update Task 13's focus area 1 description to replace 'ModelEntry.isDownloaded=true' with 'ModelEntry.downloadStatus.status == ModelDownloadStatusType.SUCCEEDED'. This is consistent with how Tasks 5, 7, 9, and 11 all describe the same check.

findings.issue: Task 15 (Test Audit) depends_on:[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,12] but not on Tasks 9, 10, 11. This means Task 15 may run before Tasks 9, 10, and 11 complete (they are also Wave 7). Test files for DrawerViewModel (Task 9), ChatTopAppBarState (Task 10), and ModelManagerViewModel (Task 11) are not listed in Task 15's audit scope. This is intentional — the test audit covers only tests from Tasks 1–8. But Task 15's dependency list missing Tasks 9–11 means the test audit cannot assess DrawerViewModelTest, ChatTopAppBarStateTest, or ModelManagerViewModelTest quality. These tests are created in Tasks 9, 10, 11 respectively and are not audited by Task 15. This is a gap in test audit coverage.
findings.fix: Either (a) add Tasks 9, 10, 11 to Task 15's depends_on and expand the audit scope to include their test files, or (b) document explicitly in Task 15 that it only covers Tasks 1–8 test files and the Wave-7 tests are out of scope for the test audit.

findings.issue: Task 6 TDD Anchor includes an instrumented test 'AppModuleCorruptionTest::testCorruptDbRenamedAndFreshDbCreated' that calls 'AppModule().provideDatabase(context, appCorruptionState, errorLog)' directly. However AppModule in Hilt is typically an abstract class or @Module and cannot be instantiated with AppModule() directly. The @Provides method for the database may be in a companion object or a non-abstract class. This tests approach may fail if the AppModule structure (abstract class with companion object @Provides, per the instruction 'follow the CoreRuntimeModule precedent') prevents direct instantiation.
findings.fix: The instrumented test should either (a) use a custom test Hilt component to get the database instance, or (b) call the @Provides method via the companion object: AppModule.Companion.provideDatabase(...), or (c) extract the database-building logic into a standalone function that is separately testable. The task should clarify the expected AppModule structure to ensure the test approach is compatible.

findings.issue: Task 3's activeModelName emits model.name (String), not model.modelId. The TDD Anchor and implementation spec consistently say 'emits the model.name of the first ModelEntry with ModelInitStatus.Ready'. But the tech-spec Decision 7 also says 'name of the model currently in ModelInitStatus.Ready state'. The ChatViewModel uses activeModelName.value for Quick/Draft mode to identify the 'active model' and the DrawerViewModel uses it for pre-navigation checks. However the DrawerViewModel checks model availability using entry.model.modelId comparison against chat.model_id (Room stores modelId). If activeModelName returns model.name but the chat row stores model.modelId, there is a semantic mismatch: DrawerViewModel compares the wrong identifier. Task 9 description correctly uses 'model_id' from registry.models, not activeModelName, for the availability check — so DrawerViewModel may be fine. But ChatViewModel in Quick/Draft mode reading 'activeModelName' (a name, not an id) to identify which model is warm could create a name-vs-id confusion when passing to WarmupCoordinator.cancelAndRestart(modelId) which expects a modelId.
findings.fix: Clarify in Task 3 spec whether activeModelName returns model.name or model.modelId. Given that Tasks 5 and 8 pass the value to registry.initialize(modelId) and warmupCoordinator.cancelAndRestart(modelId), it should return model.modelId (or be renamed activeModelId). The tech-spec Decision 7 text saying 'name' likely reflects the original Gallery architecture where name=modelId was the same value. With Task 2 separating modelId from name, this StateFlow should emit model.modelId not model.name.

findings.issue: Task 11 wave is 7 but Wave 5 per tech-spec. The YAML header says wave:7 but the tech-spec section says 'Wave 5 (depends on Wave 4)'. Tasks 9, 10, 11 all have wave:7 in YAML headers but the tech-spec calls them Wave 5. This is a consistent 2-wave offset between the tech-spec wave numbering and the YAML wave numbering throughout the task set (tech-spec Wave 1 = YAML wave 1, tech-spec Wave 2 = YAML wave 2/3, etc). Cosmetic inconsistency only — the depends_on graph is the authoritative execution order.
findings.fix: Cosmetic: align tech-spec wave labels with YAML wave numbers for readability. Not a functional issue.

findings.issue: Task 12 depends_on:[9,10,11] (wave:8), placing it after all Wave-7 tasks. Task 13 and 14 depend_on:[12] (wave:9). However Task 15 depends_on:[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,12] — it includes Tasks 1-8 and Task 12, but NOT Tasks 9, 10, 11. This means the test audit (Task 15) happens in the same wave as the code/security audits (Tasks 13, 14) — they are all wave:9, all depending on Task 12. This is fine. However, Task 16 (Pre-deploy QA) depends_on:[13,14,15] which correctly sequences it after all audits.
findings.fix: No fix needed — the wave 9/10 structure for audit and QA tasks is correct.
